---
title: "4.15 Sorting Algorithms"
subtitle: "Data Collections"
format:
  clean-revealjs:
    slide-number: true
    css: unit4-shared.css
lang: zh-CN
---

## Course Directory

### Return to the course outline

[← Back to AP CSA / 返回课程目录](../../index.html)

## Sorting Algorithms

### Sorting puts values into numeric or alphabetic order

This topic keeps two iterative AP sorting algorithms in focus:

::: {.tight-list}
- <span class="term">selection sort</span>
- <span class="term">insertion sort</span>
- merge sort is previewed here and handled fully in `4.17`
:::

## Selection Sort

### Repeatedly choose the smallest remaining value and swap it forward

Retained algorithm story:

::: {.tight-list}
- the outer loop marks the start of the unsorted region
- `minIndex` starts at the outer-loop index
- the inner loop scans the remaining unsorted values
- after the scan, swap the smallest found value into its final position
:::

## Recognizing Selection Sort

### AP questions often test pattern recognition, not just memorized code

```java
for (int j = 0; j < elements.length - 1; j++)
{
    int minIndex = j;
    for (int k = j + 1; k < elements.length; k++)
    {
        if (elements[k] < elements[minIndex]) { minIndex = k; }
    }
    // swap elements[j] and elements[minIndex]
}
```

Key signal: the selected minimum goes into its <span class="mark">final</span> location each pass.

## Insertion Sort

### Insert each new element into the already sorted left side

Retained algorithm story:

::: {.tight-list}
- the sorted region grows from left to right
- store the current value in `temp`
- shift larger values rightward
- place `temp` into the open location
:::

## Recognizing Insertion Sort

### The inner action is shifting, not choosing one minimum

```java
for (int j = 1; j < elements.length; j++)
{
    int temp = elements[j];
    int possibleIndex = j;
    while (possibleIndex > 0 && temp < elements[possibleIndex - 1])
    {
        elements[possibleIndex] = elements[possibleIndex - 1];
        possibleIndex--;
    }
    elements[possibleIndex] = temp;
}
```

Key signal: the algorithm opens space by <span class="term">shifting</span> values to the right.

## Compare the Two Sorts

### They both use nested control flow, but they improve order differently

| Algorithm | Main action | Best mental model |
|---|---|---|
| Selection sort | find min, then swap | choose the next final value |
| Insertion sort | shift, then insert | grow a sorted left side |

Retained contrast:

::: {.tight-list}
- selection sort does the same comparison pattern regardless of data order
- insertion sort can do less work when data is already mostly sorted
- both are iterative and traceable by index movement
:::

## Runtime Framing

### Both algorithms are much slower than binary-style divide-and-conquer methods

Retained conclusion:

::: {.tight-list}
- both algorithms revisit the data many times
- informal runtime comparison often comes from counting inner-loop work
- they are useful because their control flow is visible and easy to trace
:::

## Classroom Tasks

### Practice worth keeping

Retained classroom work for this topic:

::: {.tight-list}
- identify selection sort from outer-loop and `minIndex` behavior
- identify insertion sort from `temp`, `possibleIndex`, and shifting behavior
- trace one full pass of each algorithm
- compare how already sorted data affects insertion sort
- explain why selection sort still performs the same comparison structure
- <span class="term">4.15.3 Coding Challenge: Sort Runtimes</span>
:::

## Classroom Check

### A strong answer should...

::: {.tight-list}
- explain how selection sort chooses and places the next smallest value
- explain how insertion sort shifts values and inserts `temp`
- distinguish swapping from shifting
- identify the loop structure that reveals each algorithm
- compare the informal runtime behavior of the two iterative sorts
:::

## End

### Return to the course outline

[← Back to AP CSA / 返回课程目录](../../index.html)
